title: TorGuard VPN Review 2026: A Power User's VPN With Real Trade-Offs
metaDescription: Our in-depth TorGuard VPN review for 2026. We tested speeds, streaming, security, and torrenting. Find out if TorGuard's advanced features justify the price.
pros: Highly customizable with advanced protocol options (OpenVPN, WireGuard, OpenConnect, Stunnel)
Stealth VPN effectively bypasses deep packet inspection in China, Russia, and Turkey
Up to 12 simultaneous connections on Pro plan, 30 on Premium
Dedicated and residential IP addresses available for streaming and business use
Port forwarding support across all plans — great for torrenting
Browser extensions available for Chrome and Firefox
Accepts Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies for anonymous payment
cons: Based in the United States — a Five Eyes jurisdiction with FISA surveillance laws
No independent third-party security audits have ever been conducted
Streaming requires extra-cost dedicated IP add-on ($7.99/month) or Pro/Premium plan
Torrenting blocked on all US servers due to 2022 Hollywood lawsuit settlement
Inconsistent speeds — some tests showed severe slowdowns below 50 Mbps on a 500 Mbps line
Complex pricing structure with three tiers and multiple billing cycles
Only 7-day money-back guarantee — far shorter than the industry-standard 30 days
Interface feels dated and can be confusing for beginners

reviewContent:
Our Verdict

TorGuard earns a 3.4 out of 5 rating in our 2026 review. This is a VPN built for technically inclined users who need granular control over their connection settings, stealth protocols for censored regions, and reliable port forwarding for torrenting. TorGuard delivers on those niche use cases better than most competitors. But for the average VPN buyer looking for simple, fast, and streaming-friendly protection, TorGuard falls short of the market leaders.

The biggest red flags are the US jurisdiction (Five Eyes), the complete absence of independent security audits, and a confusing pricing model where streaming access costs extra on top of already mid-range prices. The 7-day money-back guarantee is stingy compared to the 30-day standard set by NordVPN, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN. That said, TorGuard has a loyal following among power users who appreciate its protocol flexibility, dedicated IP options, and the Stealth VPN features that reliably bypass the Great Firewall in China.

Security.org gives TorGuard an 8.2 out of 10 rating, while vpnMentor rates it 6.6 out of 10, and Cloudwards scores it 66 out of 100. The consensus is clear: TorGuard is a capable but imperfect VPN that excels in specific scenarios while falling behind on mainstream features.

Get TorGuard VPN from $5.00/mo →

Who Is TorGuard For?

TorGuard is a good fit if you:

  • Need Stealth VPN to bypass censorship in China, Russia, Turkey, or the UAE
  • Want port forwarding for optimized torrenting performance
  • Prefer granular control over VPN protocols and encryption settings
  • Need a dedicated or residential IP address for business or streaming
  • Use multiple devices simultaneously (up to 12 on Pro, 30 on Premium)
  • Want browser extensions for Chrome and Firefox alongside the desktop app

TorGuard is NOT for you if you:

  • Want hassle-free streaming of Netflix, Disney+, or BBC iPlayer without paying extra
  • Care about independently verified no-logs claims (TorGuard has zero audits)
  • Prefer a VPN based outside of Five Eyes surveillance jurisdiction
  • Want a simple, beginner-friendly VPN with an intuitive modern interface
  • Need a generous money-back guarantee to test the service properly
  • Want to torrent on US servers (blocked since the 2022 lawsuit settlement)

Speed Test Results

TorGuard's speed performance is inconsistent across independent reviews — a pattern that makes it difficult to recommend for users who need predictable, top-tier performance. Our research aggregated speed data from multiple trusted testing sources to paint an accurate picture.

Nearby Server Performance

According to vpnMentor's 2026 testing, TorGuard showed only a 9% speed reduction on nearby servers, which is genuinely impressive and comparable to top-tier VPNs. Using WireGuard, speeds reached up to 520 Mbps on regular server locations. However, CyberInsider's testing on a 500 Mbps connection told a different story, recording speeds of just 50 Mbps in Seattle, 47 Mbps in New York, and 23 Mbps in London — representing speed losses of 90% or more. CyberInsider also noted that WireGuard was inoperable during their testing, constantly returning connection errors.

This inconsistency is concerning. Whether you get the fast TorGuard or the slow TorGuard appears to depend on your location, the server you select, the protocol that actually works at the time, and possibly server load. There is no automatic fastest-server selection to help you find the best option.

Long-Distance Performance

vpnMentor recorded a 34% speed reduction on distant servers, which is actually competitive with many premium VPNs. However, Security.org's testing showed latency jumping to 190-193ms on distant connections with download speeds dropping to 20-40 Mbps from a baseline of approximately 48 Mbps — a 40-55% reduction.

Security & Privacy

TorGuard offers a solid set of security features on paper, but the overall privacy picture is complicated by its US jurisdiction and the absence of independent audits.

Encryption and Protocols

TorGuard uses AES-256-GCM encryption with SHA-512, which is industry-standard and effectively uncrackable. The protocol selection is where TorGuard truly stands out — it offers more options than almost any competitor:

  • OpenVPN (TCP/UDP) — the battle-tested standard
  • WireGuard — modern, fast, lightweight protocol
  • OpenConnect/AnyConnect — SSL-based VPN excellent for restrictive networks
  • IKEv2/IPSec — fast mobile connections
  • Stunnel — wraps OpenVPN in SSL to evade deep packet inspection
  • Shadowsocks — built-in proxy protocol for bypassing censorship
  • L2TP/IPSec — legacy protocol for compatibility

This extensive protocol support is one of TorGuard's genuine advantages. The Stealth VPN feature, which disguises VPN traffic as regular HTTPS, is particularly valuable for users in China, Russia, Turkey, and corporate networks that actively block VPN connections.

No Independent Audits — A Major Gap

TorGuard has never undergone an independent third-party security audit. In an industry where NordVPN has completed six no-logs audits (by PwC and Deloitte), ExpressVPN has been audited by Cure53 and KPMG, and even smaller players like Mullvad have eight audits under their belt, TorGuard's absence from the audit table is a significant trust gap.

TorGuard claims a strict zero-logs policy, stating it does not monitor, record, or store any online activities, browsing history, or connection data. However, without independent verification, this remains an unproven claim. The company does operate RAM-only servers, meaning no data is written to disk — a positive architectural decision that limits the potential for data retention even if something went wrong.

The 2022 Lawsuit — A Real-World Privacy Test

In March 2022, TorGuard settled a copyright infringement lawsuit filed by over two dozen Hollywood film studios. The studios had sent 97,640 piracy notices to TorGuard's hosting provider, QuadraNet. As part of the settlement, TorGuard agreed to block all BitTorrent traffic on US servers. Crucially, no identifiable user logs were produced during the legal proceedings, which provides some real-world evidence that TorGuard's no-logs policy held up under legal pressure — though this is not the same as a formal audit.

US Jurisdiction Concerns

TorGuard is headquartered in Orlando, Florida, placing it firmly within US jurisdiction and the Five Eyes intelligence-sharing alliance. The United States is subject to FISA (Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act), National Security Letters, and gag orders that can compel companies to collect user data secretly. While TorGuard's RAM-only servers and no-logs claims mitigate this risk, the jurisdictional concern is real and legitimate. Privacy-focused competitors like Mullvad (Sweden, outside direct US control), Proton VPN (Switzerland), and NordVPN (Panama) all benefit from more privacy-friendly jurisdictions.

Leak Protection

On the positive side, independent testing confirms that TorGuard passes DNS leak tests, WebRTC leak tests, and IPv6 leak tests without any failures. The kill switch functions as advertised, immediately cutting internet access when the VPN connection drops. TorGuard also offers an "App Kill" feature that selectively terminates specific applications if the VPN disconnects — a useful granular option not found in many competitors.

Streaming Performance

Streaming is TorGuard's most frustrating area. The service can technically unblock Netflix and some other platforms, but only if you pay extra for dedicated streaming IP addresses or subscribe to the more expensive Pro or Premium plans.

The Streaming IP Problem

On the Standard plan ($9.99/month), TorGuard's shared IP addresses are blocked by Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, Hulu, and most other streaming platforms. To access streaming content, you need either:

  • The Pro plan ($12.99/month), which includes one streaming IP
  • The Premium plan ($16.49/month), which also includes a streaming IP
  • A standalone streaming IP add-on at $7.99/month on top of any plan

This means streaming-capable TorGuard costs $12.99-17.98/month — significantly more than NordVPN ($3.39/month on a 2-year plan) or Surfshark ($2.41/month), both of which include streaming unblocking at no extra cost.

Streaming Test Results

With a dedicated streaming IP on the Pro plan, Security.org reported successfully accessing Netflix libraries in 15+ countries, as well as Amazon Prime Video. However, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and Hulu remained blocked in testing by multiple independent reviewers.

The Bottom Line on Streaming

If streaming geo-restricted content is important to you, TorGuard is a poor value proposition. You are paying more for less reliable access compared to NordVPN or Surfshark, which both unblock 30+ Netflix libraries and most major streaming platforms at a fraction of the price.

Pricing & Plans

TorGuard's pricing structure is more complex than most VPN providers. There are three tiers (Standard, Pro, Premium), each available on monthly, quarterly, semi-annual, annual, 2-year, and 3-year billing cycles. The official pricing page confirms the following rates as of March 2026:

Current Pricing (March 2026)

PlanMonthlyAnnualDevicesStreaming IP

Pro$12.99/mo$69.99/yr ($5.83/mo)121 included

Optional Add-Ons

  • Streaming IP: $7.99/month — dedicated IP optimized for Netflix and other platforms (available for US, UK, France, Germany, Spain, Japan)
  • Residential IP: $13.99/month — appears as a regular home ISP connection

How It Compares on Price

TorGuard's Standard annual plan at $5.00/month is competitive in isolation. But once you add a streaming IP ($7.99/month) to match the feature set of NordVPN or Surfshark, the cost rises to $12.99/month — far more expensive than NordVPN's $3.39/month (2-year Plus plan) or Surfshark's $2.41/month (2-year Starter plan). The Pro plan at $5.83/month annual includes a streaming IP, making it the best value if you need streaming access, but it still cannot match the market leaders on price-to-feature ratio.

Money-Back Guarantee

TorGuard offers only a 7-day money-back guarantee — the shortest in the mainstream VPN industry. NordVPN, Surfshark, ExpressVPN, CyberGhost, and most competitors all offer 30-45 day guarantees. Seven days is barely enough time to test a VPN thoroughly across different use cases, servers, and network conditions.

Get TorGuard VPN from $5.00/mo →

Apps & Usability

TorGuard provides native apps for all major platforms:

  • Desktop: Windows, macOS, Linux
  • Mobile: Android, iOS
  • Browser extensions: Chrome and Firefox (SSL proxy-based)
  • Router support: DD-WRT, Tomato, pfSense

Interface and Experience

The TorGuard desktop app is functional but visually dated compared to the polished interfaces of NordVPN or Surfshark. Cloudwards described it as "difficult to use" with a dated interface, and this matches the consensus across most reviews. The app exposes a large number of technical settings — protocol selection, encryption cipher, port configuration, DNS settings, proxy options — which power users will appreciate but beginners may find overwhelming.

There is no world map interface, no automatic "fastest server" selection, and the server list requires manual browsing. For experienced VPN users who know exactly what they want, this is fine. For someone who just wants to click "connect" and be protected, the experience is subpar.

Simultaneous Connections

TorGuard offers generous device limits compared to many competitors:

  • Standard: 8 devices
  • Pro: 12 devices
  • Premium: 30 devices

This is a meaningful advantage. NordVPN allows 10 devices, ExpressVPN allows 8, and Mullvad only 5. Only Surfshark (unlimited) beats TorGuard's Premium plan on device count.

Torrenting Features

Despite the brand name ("Tor" in TorGuard originally referred to torrenting, not the Tor network), TorGuard's torrenting story is complicated. Port forwarding is available across all plans, which is a significant advantage for optimizing torrent download speeds. SOCKS5 proxy support is also included. However, following the 2022 lawsuit settlement, all BitTorrent traffic is blocked on US servers. Torrenting still works on servers in all other countries, but if you are US-based and want to use a nearby server for maximum speed, you will need to connect to Canada, Mexico, or another non-US location.

What I Don't Like

After extensive research and analysis, here are the honest negatives that temper our recommendation of TorGuard:

1. Zero Independent Audits

In 2026, there is no excuse for a major VPN provider to have never undergone an independent security audit. NordVPN has done six, Mullvad has done eight, ExpressVPN has done multiple — and TorGuard has done zero. The 2022 lawsuit provides some circumstantial evidence that the no-logs policy holds, but that is not a substitute for a professional audit by a firm like Deloitte, PwC, or Cure53.

2. US Jurisdiction Is a Liability

The United States has the legal authority to compel companies to collect user data through National Security Letters and FISA court orders, complete with gag provisions that prevent the company from disclosing the surveillance. For a product whose entire value proposition is privacy, being based in the US is a fundamental weakness.

3. Speed Inconsistency

The wild variation between speed test results from different reviewers — 520 Mbps in one test, 23 Mbps in another — suggests that TorGuard's server network has significant quality variance. WireGuard not working at all during CyberInsider's testing is particularly concerning. You should not have to hope you get a good server.

4. Streaming Costs Extra

Charging $7.99/month on top of the VPN subscription for streaming access — a feature that NordVPN and Surfshark include for free at half the total price — feels out of step with the market in 2026. The Standard plan's inability to unblock any streaming platform makes it feel like an incomplete product.

5. The 7-Day Money-Back Guarantee

Seven days is not enough time to properly evaluate a VPN. You need time to test different servers, protocols, and use cases across different network conditions. The industry standard is 30 days for good reason.

Who Should Choose TorGuard?

TorGuard is a niche product for niche users. Here is when it makes sense:

The Censorship Bypasser

If you live in or travel to China, Russia, Turkey, Iran, or the UAE and need reliable VPN access, TorGuard's Stealth VPN with Stunnel, Shadowsocks, and OpenConnect provides some of the most comprehensive anti-censorship tools available. The WStunnel feature is specifically designed to defeat deep packet inspection.

The Power User / Tinkerer

If you want to manually configure protocols, encryption ciphers, ports, and DNS settings, TorGuard gives you more knobs to turn than almost any other consumer VPN. The App Kill feature, custom scripts support, and protocol diversity are genuinely impressive for technically inclined users.

The Torrenter (Outside the US)

With port forwarding on all plans, SOCKS5 proxy support, and no bandwidth limits, TorGuard remains a strong torrenting VPN — as long as you connect to servers outside the United States. The US server torrent block, while understandable given the lawsuit, is a real limitation for US-based users.

The Multi-Device Household

With 12 devices on Pro and 30 on Premium, TorGuard supports more simultaneous connections than NordVPN (10) or ExpressVPN (8). For families or small offices, this could be a deciding factor.

Everyone Else: Look Elsewhere

For mainstream users who want fast speeds, easy streaming, verified privacy, and a modern app experience, NordVPN or Surfshark are better choices at lower prices. TorGuard's advantages are real but narrow, and its disadvantages affect the majority of typical VPN use cases.

Try TorGuard VPN with 7-day money-back guarantee →

FAQ

Is TorGuard VPN safe to use in 2026?

TorGuard uses AES-256 encryption, supports multiple secure protocols including WireGuard and OpenVPN, and operates RAM-only servers. Independent DNS and IP leak tests confirm no leaks. However, TorGuard has never undergone an independent security audit, and it is based in the United States under Five Eyes jurisdiction. It is functionally safe for everyday use, but privacy purists should consider alternatives like Mullvad or Proton VPN that have verified no-logs policies.

Does TorGuard work with Netflix?

Yes, but only on the Pro or Premium plan, which include a dedicated streaming IP. The Standard plan cannot unblock Netflix on any server. You can also add a streaming IP to any plan for $7.99/month extra. Supported Netflix libraries include the US, UK, France, Germany, Spain, and Japan. Competitors like NordVPN and Surfshark unblock Netflix on all plans at lower prices.

Why can't I torrent on TorGuard US servers?

In March 2022, TorGuard settled a copyright infringement lawsuit filed by major Hollywood studios. As part of the settlement, TorGuard agreed to block all BitTorrent traffic on its US servers. Torrenting still works on servers in all other countries. No identifiable user data was produced during the lawsuit, which suggests the no-logs policy held up under legal pressure.

How many devices can I use with TorGuard?

The Standard plan supports 8 simultaneous connections, the Pro plan supports 12, and the Premium plan supports 30. This is more generous than NordVPN (10 devices) and ExpressVPN (8 devices), though Surfshark offers unlimited connections on all plans.

Does TorGuard work in China?

Yes. TorGuard's Stealth VPN feature, which uses Stunnel, Shadowsocks, and OpenConnect protocols, can bypass China's Great Firewall. The AnyConnect protocol is specifically recommended for Chinese users. However, no VPN can guarantee 100% reliability in China, as the Great Firewall is constantly updated.

Has TorGuard been audited?

No. As of March 2026, TorGuard has never undergone an independent third-party security audit. This is a significant gap compared to competitors like NordVPN (6 audits by PwC and Deloitte), ExpressVPN (audited by Cure53 and KPMG), and Mullvad (8 audits by multiple firms). TorGuard's no-logs claim is unverified by any external party.

Is TorGuard good for torrenting?

TorGuard was originally designed with torrenting in mind and still offers strong P2P features: port forwarding on all plans, SOCKS5 proxy support, and no bandwidth limits. However, BitTorrent traffic is blocked on all US servers due to a 2022 legal settlement. If you are based in the US, you will need to connect to non-US servers (like Canada) for torrenting, which may reduce speeds.

What is the cheapest TorGuard plan?

The cheapest option is the Standard annual plan at $59.99/year ($5.00/month). However, this plan does not include streaming support or a dedicated IP. For streaming, the Pro annual plan at $69.99/year ($5.83/month) is the best value as it includes one streaming IP. Additional multi-year billing options are available with further discounts.
